title: Of Mice And Tony

jokes.text: I'm at the crossroads of my life and I'm a little scared... and you're standin' here with this goofy grin on your face.
jokes.setup: Tony wants to discuss a serious personal problem
jokes.punchline: Alex is visibly distracted by his own good fortune, undermining the moment
jokes.explanation: Good character moment - Tony's earnestness contrasted with Alex's obliviousness. Well-structured setup/payoff.

jokes.text: Did I stop yet?
jokes.setup: Alex promises to stop grinning
jokes.punchline: He's still grinning and has to ask if he stopped
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective physical comedy. Great economy - maximum humor in minimal words.

jokes.text: You know, losin' every game is a new garage record.
jokes.setup: Louie lost every single football bet
jokes.punchline: Treating complete failure as a noteworthy achievement
jokes.explanation: Nice deadpan observation that reframes failure as accomplishment. Good quotability.

jokes.text: I never thought anything would make me feel sorry for Louie... and I was right.
jokes.setup: Sets up expectation that Alex might feel sympathy for Louie
jokes.punchline: Immediately subverts it - he still doesn't feel sorry
jokes.explanation: Excellent misdirection with perfect timing. Classic setup-and-subvert structure with great character consistency.

jokes.text: You all like Ignatowski? Shut up or he's a cocker spaniel.
jokes.setup: Louie makes a seemingly innocent question about Jim
jokes.punchline: Threatens to turn him into a dog if they respond
jokes.explanation: Wonderfully absurd threat that fits Louie's petty tyrannical character perfectly. The specificity of 'cocker spaniel' elevates it.

jokes.text: No brains, no pain.
jokes.setup: Plays on the common phrase 'no pain, no gain'
jokes.punchline: Jim's version suggests his lack of intelligence protects him from suffering
jokes.explanation: Perfect character line for Jim - simple wordplay that captures his childlike wisdom. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: She's not guys.
jokes.setup: Louie is invoking the 'guys' code' to pressure Alex into another bet
jokes.punchline: Dismisses Elaine's advice because she's 'not guys' - using 'guys' as a noun
jokes.explanation: Funny grammatical construction that shows Louie's desperation and crude worldview.

jokes.text: I want Houston and Oakland.
jokes.setup: Louie keeps changing his mind about which team to pick
jokes.punchline: Finally demands to bet on both teams, which is impossible
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Louie's panic and poor gambling instincts. Shows his desperation mounting.

jokes.text: Hut, hut, hut, hut. Hut.
jokes.setup: Louie retreats to his dispatcher cage
jokes.punchline: Makes football quarterback sounds while scurrying away
jokes.explanation: Physical comedy beat that shows Louie's childish retreat after making the bet.

jokes.text: Commie judges.
jokes.setup: Tony explains why his fighter lost the Olympic final to a Cuban
jokes.punchline: Blames communist judges rather than the fighter's performance
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Tony's simple worldview and loyalty to his fighter. Very economical.

jokes.text: That's silly. Silly? Okay. Thanks a lot, Alex. You bet. Ow!
jokes.setup: Alex dismisses Tony's concerns as silly
jokes.punchline: Tony's hurt reaction and Alex's oblivious 'you bet' followed by apparent physical retaliation
jokes.explanation: Nice beat showing Alex's social tone-deafness. The 'Ow!' suggests Tony's physical response to being dismissed.

jokes.text: Would you like me to go home and wash my hair?
jokes.setup: Tony asks Tommy to make the place look classy
jokes.punchline: Tommy offers to wash his clearly disheveled hair as the solution
jokes.explanation: Good self-aware humor from Tommy about his own appearance. Visual element adds to the comedy.

jokes.text: I don't think there's time for that, Tom.
jokes.setup: Tommy offers to wash his hair to look classier
jokes.punchline: Tony politely declines as if it's a reasonable suggestion but impractical
jokes.explanation: Tony's earnest response to an absurd suggestion. Good straight-man delivery.

jokes.text: Thanks for the 'Mr. Banta.'
jokes.setup: Tommy is helping Tony look impressive by calling him 'Mr. Banta'
jokes.punchline: Tony awkwardly acknowledges the performance, breaking character
jokes.explanation: Cringe moment where Tony can't help but acknowledge the artifice of the situation.

jokes.text: And, uh, here's the phone you always have on your table when you eat here.
jokes.setup: Tommy is trying to make Tony look like a big shot
jokes.punchline: Brings a phone as a prop, awkwardly explaining its purpose
jokes.explanation: Good visual gag with the phone prop. The awkward explanation adds to the comedy.

jokes.text: Banta Management... Oh, it was personal.
jokes.setup: Tony answers the fake phone call to look important
jokes.punchline: When asked who called, he mysteriously says it was 'personal'
jokes.explanation: Tony trying to maintain the illusion of being a busy manager. Good cringe comedy.

jokes.text: I just hope he can fight better than he talks.
jokes.setup: Tony has just finished his pitch to manage Terry
jokes.punchline: Tommy observes that Terry wasn't very articulate during the conversation
jokes.explanation: Sharp observation that deflates the moment. Good economy and timing.

jokes.text: When? When the pretzels start dancin'.
jokes.setup: Jim claims he knows when to stop drinking
jokes.punchline: His indicator is when pretzels start dancing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim line - absurd, specific, and reveals his unique relationship with reality. Highly quotable.

jokes.text: When they all start dancing... the fox-trot.
jokes.setup: Jim has said he stops drinking when pretzels dance
jokes.punchline: Adds the specific detail that they do the fox-trot
jokes.explanation: Beautiful escalation that adds an even more specific absurd detail. The fox-trot is perfectly old-fashioned for Jim.

jokes.text: Oh, quite all right, Tommy. Set me up with a pint, would you? Oh, that's a good fellow.
jokes.setup: Jim has switched to an upper-class British persona
jokes.punchline: Orders a pint in a posh accent as if he's at a British pub
jokes.explanation: Jim's British persona shift is perfectly in character. The posh language contrasts with the working-class bar setting.

jokes.text: Could we change the station on the telly, please? Uh, Masterpiece Theater is beginning an adaptation of The Moon and Sixpence. Need I say more?
jokes.setup: Jim wants to change the TV channel from the football game
jokes.punchline: He wants to watch highbrow British literature adaptation instead
jokes.explanation: Great character comedy - Jim's pretentious British persona wanting culture instead of sports. Good literary reference.

jokes.text: Oh, Alistair, I'm mad for your tailor.
jokes.setup: Jim is watching Masterpiece Theater
jokes.punchline: Comments on the show's character's clothing in an affected upper-class way
jokes.explanation: Jim getting absorbed in the pretentious show. The affected language is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: Oh, Alistair, I'm mad for your tailor.
jokes.setup: British Latka commenting on TV character's clothing
jokes.punchline: Absurdly affected commentary on television fashion
jokes.explanation: Continues the British persona with appropriately pretentious commentary. The phrase 'mad for your tailor' is perfectly affected.

jokes.text: I consider this a breach of good taste.
jokes.setup: Louie has changed the channel back to football
jokes.punchline: Jim treats it as a serious violation of etiquette
jokes.explanation: Jim's pompous reaction to having his show changed. Good character consistency.

jokes.text: Go back to merry old nutland.
jokes.setup: Jim is acting pretentiously British
jokes.punchline: Louie tells him to go back to 'nutland' instead of England
jokes.explanation: Louie's crude but effective putdown. Good wordplay on 'merry old England.'

jokes.text: Go back to merry old nutland.
jokes.setup: Louie's response to British Latka's complaints
jokes.punchline: Clever twist on 'merry old England' insulting both the persona and Latka's mental state
jokes.explanation: Excellent Louie line combining wordplay with character-specific insult. 'Merry old nutland' is perfectly crafted.

jokes.text: There's still such a thing as a strong letter to the Times, you know.
jokes.setup: Jim is offended by Louie's behavior
jokes.punchline: Threatens to write a letter to the newspaper as his form of retaliation
jokes.explanation: Perfect Jim line - his idea of serious retaliation is writing to a newspaper. Great character-specific threat.

jokes.text: Construction.
jokes.setup: Terry's parents worry boxing might hurt his hands and affect his other job
jokes.punchline: His other job is construction work, which is also hard on hands
jokes.explanation: Perfect ironic revelation. Maximum economy - one word delivers the entire joke. Great structure.

jokes.text: Well, it's, uh, something to think about.
jokes.setup: Terry reveals his other job is construction
jokes.punchline: Elaine's understated reaction to the obvious irony
jokes.explanation: Good straight-woman response that acknowledges the absurdity without overplaying it.

jokes.text: My grandmother could've kicked it better than that dumb Rumanian!
jokes.setup: The field goal kicker missed and Louie lost his bet
jokes.punchline: Louie blames the kicker's ethnicity and compares him to his grandmother
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie outburst with ethnic stereotyping. In character but not particularly clever.

jokes.text: My life is manure!
jokes.setup: Louie has lost another big bet
jokes.punchline: Declares his entire existence is manure
jokes.explanation: Classic Louie melodrama. The specific word choice of 'manure' is perfect for his character.

jokes.text: Would you feel better if I gave you the money you just lost? Of course I would. Well, too bad. I don't have it.
jokes.setup: Jim offers to give Louie the money he lost
jokes.punchline: Gets Louie's hopes up then immediately dashes them
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection setup. Jim's mock kindness followed by immediate deflation works well.

jokes.text: You pulled my ear. I pulled your leg.
jokes.setup: Louie physically grabbed Jim's ear in anger
jokes.punchline: Jim points out the literal vs metaphorical 'pulling'
jokes.explanation: Excellent wordplay with perfect economy. The literal/metaphorical contrast is well-executed and quotable.

jokes.text: Oh, some crummy place, couldn't even afford menus. I mean, the guy came around and told you what you got to eat.
jokes.setup: Terry describes the fancy restaurant the syndicate took him to
jokes.punchline: He thinks having a waiter explain the menu means they can't afford printed menus
jokes.explanation: Brilliant misunderstanding of upscale dining. Terry's working-class perspective interprets luxury as poverty.

jokes.text: So what'd you tell 'em? I went for the veal.
jokes.setup: Tony asks what Terry told the syndicate about signing with them
jokes.punchline: Terry thinks he's asking about his food order
jokes.explanation: Good misdirection playing on Terry's simple mindset. The miscommunication feels natural.

jokes.text: Well, I don't want 'em here. I already got 'em tickets.
jokes.setup: Tony says he doesn't want the syndicate at the fight
jokes.punchline: Terry has already bought them tickets
jokes.explanation: Good timing on the revelation. Terry's innocent undermining of Tony's authority.

jokes.text: Just when I was gettin' used to saying, 'Give him air, give him air.'
jokes.setup: Alex notes the difference between seconding for Tony vs Terry
jokes.punchline: References Tony's habit of getting knocked out
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Tony's boxing career. Self-deprecating humor that feels earned.

jokes.text: like I had a albacore hangin' around my neck.
jokes.setup: Tony talks about feeling jinxed in life
jokes.punchline: Confuses 'albatross' with 'albacore' (a type of tuna)
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tony malapropism. The fish confusion is exactly right for his character and very quotable.

jokes.text: I got $5,200 in cash, Reiger. I got $2,400 here, I got $2,400 here, I got a hundred dollars here and a hundred dollars here and $200 in Chase Manhattan.
jokes.setup: Louie shows Alex he has cash to bet
jokes.punchline: Obsessively details exactly where each amount is kept
jokes.explanation: Shows Louie's obsessive nature and gambling desperation. The detailed accounting is very character-specific but runs a bit long.

jokes.text: How long's Banta been training him? A couple of weeks. Please!
jokes.setup: Alex warns Louie that Tony's fighter is good
jokes.punchline: When Louie learns Tony's only been training him for weeks, he dismisses the threat
jokes.explanation: Good setup for Louie's overconfidence. His dismissive 'Please!' is perfectly in character.

jokes.text: They're gone, God. Let's talk bucks! Bucks!
jokes.setup: Louie has just said a seemingly sincere prayer before the fight
jokes.punchline: Immediately drops the act and appeals to God about money
jokes.explanation: Excellent character moment showing Louie's complete lack of genuine spirituality. Perfect timing and delivery.

jokes.text: Don't say ootch! Get him.
jokes.setup: Tony is coaching Terry during the boxing match
jokes.punchline: Corrects himself for making a weird sound while coaching
jokes.explanation: Good moment of Tony's self-awareness during an exciting moment. Shows his earnestness.

jokes.text: That was quite a knockout. I've never seen anybody go out like that and I'm an anesthesiologist.
jokes.setup: The anesthesiologist comments on Terry being knocked out
jokes.punchline: As someone who regularly knocks people out professionally, he's impressed
jokes.explanation: Clever professional joke that uses the doctor's expertise for humor. Well-structured punchline.

jokes.text: How about $5,000? That's plenty.
jokes.setup: Tony gives an impassioned speech about his dreams being priceless
jokes.punchline: Immediately accepts the first offer made
jokes.explanation: Excellent deflation of Tony's grand speech. Perfect timing and shows his practical side beneath the dreams.

jokes.text: Take it and die! Die! Die! Die! Die! Die! Die with festering boil. Die! Die! Die!
jokes.setup: Louie is paying Alex his gambling debt
jokes.punchline: Goes into an escalating death wish rant while handing over money
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie meltdown with escalating curses. The 'festering boil' detail is nicely specific.

jokes.text: What a guy.
jokes.setup: Terry has just left after their emotional goodbye
jokes.punchline: Tony's simple, heartfelt reaction to Terry's loyalty
jokes.explanation: Perfect economy and character moment. Tony's simple appreciation shows his genuine nature.

jokes.text: Where do you want to eat? How about Mario's? Perfect.
jokes.setup: Tony and Alex have $10,000 to celebrate with
jokes.punchline: Tony still wants to go to the same humble restaurant
jokes.explanation: Nice callback that shows Tony hasn't been changed by his windfall. Character consistency is rewarded.

tmdb_overview: Tony becomes the manager of a promising young heavyweight boxer at his gym, but after the boxer wins a major fight, the ""syndicate"" (which turns out to be some doctors looking for an investment, rather than the mob) makes Tony an offer he can't refuse and takes over the boxer's management.
insight: Tony's mouse subplot drives 47 jokes across 44 minutes—pure character escalation at Taxi's sweet spot.
